questions.question: What is the primary characteristic that determines whether a server should be implemented as iterative or concurrent?
questions.option_a: A. The number of clients expected to connect simultaneously
questions.option_b: B. Whether the time to service a request depends on the request itself
questions.option_c: C. The operating system being used
questions.option_d: D. The port number being used
questions.explanation: When the amount of time to service a request depends on the request itself (unknown ahead of time), the server should handle it concurrently; otherwise, the server may be tied up with one client while others wait.

questions.question: What value does fork() return in the child process?
questions.option_a: A. The process ID of the child
questions.option_b: B. The process ID of the parent
questions.explanation: fork() returns 0 in the child process, the process ID of the child in the parent, and -1 on error.

questions.question: In a typical concurrent server using fork(), which socket does the child process close immediately after fork() returns?
questions.option_a: A. The connected socket (connfd)
questions.option_b: B. The listening socket (listenfd)
questions.option_c: C. Both listening and connected sockets
questions.explanation: The child process closes the listening socket immediately because it only needs the connected socket to communicate with the client.

questions.question: What happens to file descriptor reference counts after fork() is called?
questions.option_a: A. They remain the same as before fork()
questions.option_b: B. They are reset to 1 for all descriptors
questions.option_c: C. They are incremented by 1 for each shared descriptor
questions.option_d: D. They are decremented by 1 for each shared descriptor
questions.explanation: When fork() returns, all open descriptors in the parent are duplicated in the child, causing the reference count for each descriptor to be incremented by 1.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the INADDR_ANY constant in the bind() call?
questions.option_a: A. To bind to a specific IP address
questions.option_b: B. To bind to the loopback interface only
questions.option_c: C. To accept connections destined for any local interface on a multihomed system
questions.explanation: INADDR_ANY tells the system to accept connections destined for any local interface, which is useful when the system has multiple network interfaces.

questions.question: What does the readline() function return when it receives a FIN from the client?
questions.option_b: B. The number of bytes read
questions.option_d: D. The FIN flag is set in the return value
questions.explanation: readline() returns 0 when it receives a FIN, indicating end-of-file (the connection was closed by the other end).

questions.question: What is a zombie process?
questions.option_a: A. A process that is currently running
questions.option_b: B. A process that has terminated but whose parent has not yet fetched its termination status
questions.option_c: C. A process that has been stopped by a signal
questions.option_d: D. A process that has no parent
questions.explanation: A zombie process is one that has terminated but still has an entry in the process table because its parent hasn't called wait() to fetch its termination status.

questions.question: What signal is sent to a parent process when one of its child processes terminates?
questions.option_a: A. SIGTERM
questions.option_b: B. SIGINT
questions.option_c: C. SIGCHLD
questions.explanation: SIGCHLD is sent by the kernel to the parent process whenever one of its child processes terminates.

questions.question: Which two signals cannot be caught or ignored?
questions.option_a: A. SIGTERM and SIGINT
questions.option_b: B. SIGCHLD and SIGPIPE
questions.option_c: C. SIGKILL and SIGSTOP
questions.explanation: SIGKILL and SIGSTOP cannot be caught, blocked, or ignored. They always perform their default action.

questions.question: What error does accept() return when interrupted by a signal handler?
questions.option_a: A. EAGAIN
questions.option_b: B. EINTR
questions.option_c: C. ECONNABORTED
questions.explanation: When a process is blocked in a slow system call like accept() and a signal handler returns, the system call can return the EINTR error (interrupted system call).

questions.question: What happens to a process that writes to a socket that has received an RST?
questions.option_a: A. The write operation succeeds normally
questions.option_b: B. The write operation blocks indefinitely
questions.option_c: C. The SIGPIPE signal is sent to the process
questions.option_d: D. The socket is automatically reconnected
questions.explanation: When a process writes to a socket that has received an RST, the SIGPIPE signal is sent to the process. The default action of SIGPIPE is to terminate the process.

questions.question: What is the main difference between wait() and waitpid() with the WNOHANG option?
questions.option_a: A. wait() can specify which child to wait for, waitpid() cannot
questions.option_b: B. wait() blocks if there are running children, while waitpid() with WNOHANG returns 0 if no terminated child
questions.option_c: C. wait() returns immediately, waitpid() always blocks
questions.explanation: wait() blocks the calling process when there are running children, while waitpid() with the WNOHANG option returns 0 immediately if there are no terminated children.

questions.question: Why is wait() insufficient for handling multiple simultaneous SIGCHLD signals?
questions.option_a: A. wait() cannot handle child termination at all
questions.option_b: B. wait() blocks the parent process
questions.option_c: C. UNIX signals are normally not queued, so multiple signals may be delivered as only one
questions.option_d: D. wait() can only be called once
questions.explanation: UNIX signals are normally not queued. If multiple SIGCHLD signals are generated before the signal handler executes, they may be delivered as only one signal, causing wait() to only handle one child.

questions.question: What error does connect() return when the server host is not running?
questions.option_a: A. ETIMEDOUT
questions.option_b: B. ECONNREFUSED
questions.option_c: C. EHOSTUNREACH
questions.explanation: When the server is not running, the server TCP responds to the client's SYN with an RST, causing the client's connect() to return ECONNREFUSED.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the SA_RESTART flag in sigaction()?
questions.option_a: A. To restart the process after it terminates
questions.option_b: B. To automatically restart interrupted system calls after signal handler returns
questions.option_c: C. To restart the signal handler
questions.option_d: D. To prevent signals from being delivered
questions.explanation: The SA_RESTART flag causes certain system calls interrupted by a signal to be automatically restarted by the kernel after the signal handler returns.

questions.question: In the TCP echo server example, what function does the child process call to handle client requests?
questions.option_a: A. str_cli()
questions.option_b: B. str_echo()
questions.option_c: C. echo_line()
questions.explanation: The child process calls str_echo() to read lines from the client and echo them back. This function reads from the socket and writes back the same data.

questions.question: What happens when a client connects to a server but never sends a request in an iterative server?
questions.option_a: A. The server automatically times out and closes the connection
questions.option_b: B. The server continues to handle other clients normally
questions.option_c: C. The server deadlocks, unable to handle other clients
questions.option_d: D. The client is forced to send data
questions.explanation: In an iterative server, if a client connects but never sends a request, the server deadlocks because it's blocked waiting for input from that client and cannot accept new connections.

questions.question: What is the correct way to handle accept() returning EINTR?
questions.option_a: A. Exit the program
questions.option_b: B. Ignore the error and continue
questions.option_c: C. Call accept() again in a loop with continue
questions.option_d: D. Close the socket and create a new one
questions.explanation: When accept() returns EINTR due to an interrupted system call, the correct handling is to continue the loop and call accept() again, as shown in the example: if (errno == EINTR) continue;

questions.question: What is the state of a connection after the server process crashes but before the client sends more data?
questions.option_a: A. The connection is immediately terminated
questions.option_b: B. The client's TCP connection remains half-open
questions.option_c: C. The connection is automatically reset
questions.option_d: D. The server's TCP sends a FIN immediately
questions.explanation: When a server process crashes, the kernel closes all descriptors, sending FIN to the client. The client's connection becomes half-open, still able to send data but not receive, until it attempts to write.

questions.question: What error does readline() return when it receives an RST before reading?
questions.option_a: A. ETIMEDOUT
questions.option_b: B. ECONNRESET
questions.option_c: C. EPIPE
questions.explanation: If an RST arrives before readline() reads data, the call returns the ECONNRESET error, which means 'Connection reset by peer'.

questions.question: Why is passing binary data structures directly over sockets problematic across different architectures?
questions.option_a: A. Binary data cannot be transmitted over TCP
questions.option_b: B. Different byte orders and sizes of data types between hosts
questions.option_c: C. Sockets only support text data
questions.option_d: D. Binary data is slower to transmit
questions.explanation: Passing binary data directly can fail when hosts have different byte orders (big-endian vs little-endian) or different sizes for data types like long integers.

questions.question: What is the recommended solution for handling different data representations across hosts?
questions.option_a: A. Always use binary data for efficiency
questions.option_b: B. Pass all numeric data as text strings or define explicit binary formats
questions.option_c: C. Use the same architecture for all hosts
questions.option_d: D. Compress all data before transmission
questions.explanation: To ensure interoperability, either pass all numeric data as text strings or explicitly define binary formats (bit counts, endianness) for all exchanged data.

questions.question: What does the sig_chld signal handler in the lecture use to prevent zombies when multiple children terminate simultaneously?
questions.option_a: A. A single call to wait()
questions.option_b: B. A loop with waitpid() and the WNOHANG option
questions.option_c: C. Ignoring SIGCHLD
questions.explanation: The correct signal handler uses a while loop with waitpid(-1, &stat, WNOHANG) to reap all terminated children without blocking, preventing zombies even when multiple children terminate at once.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the listen() function in a TCP server?
questions.option_a: A. To establish a connection with a client
questions.option_b: B. To mark the socket as passive to accept incoming connection requests
questions.option_c: C. To bind the socket to an address
questions.option_d: D. To receive data from clients
questions.explanation: listen() marks the socket referred to by the file descriptor as a passive socket that will be used to accept incoming connection requests using accept().

questions.question: What is the return value of fork() in the parent process on success?
questions.option_b: B. The process ID of the parent
questions.option_c: C. The process ID of the child
questions.explanation: On success, fork() returns the process ID of the child process in the parent, and returns 0 in the child.

questions.question: What happens to a process's open file descriptors after a call to exec()?
questions.option_a: A. All descriptors are closed
questions.option_b: B. The process ID changes
questions.option_c: C. The new program inherits copies of the open descriptors
questions.option_d: D. Only standard input/output are preserved
questions.explanation: exec() replaces the current process image with a new program, but the process ID does not change, and the new program inherits copies of the open file descriptors.

questions.question: In the concurrent server example, what happens if the parent fails to close the connected socket (connfd)?
questions.option_a: A. The connection is automatically closed after a timeout
questions.option_b: B. The parent may run out of descriptors and client connections won't terminate properly
questions.option_c: C. The child process closes it automatically
questions.option_d: D. Nothing, it's not necessary to close it
questions.explanation: If the parent doesn't close the connected socket, reference counts never reach 0, preventing proper connection termination, and the parent may eventually run out of file descriptors.

questions.question: What does the bzero() function do in the TCP server example?
questions.option_a: A. It binds the socket to an address
questions.option_b: B. It zeros out the entire socket address structure
questions.option_c: C. It creates a new socket
questions.explanation: bzero() is used to set the entire servaddr socket address structure object to zero before filling in the address family, IP address, and port number.

questions.question: What is a 'slow system call'?
questions.option_a: A. Any system call that takes more than 1 second to execute
questions.option_b: B. System calls that can block forever, such as accept(), read(), and write()
questions.option_c: C. System calls that are deprecated
questions.option_d: D. System calls that require root privileges
questions.explanation: Slow system calls are those that can block forever, such as accept(), read(), write(), and select(). These calls are particularly affected by signal interruptions.

questions.question: What error occurs when a client's connect() call times out due to no network connectivity?
questions.option_a: A. ECONNREFUSED
questions.option_b: B. EHOSTUNREACH
questions.option_c: C. ETIMEDOUT
questions.explanation: If there is no network connectivity and no ICMP error is returned, connect() will eventually return ETIMEDOUT after the system's timeout period (typically 75 seconds).

questions.question: What is the default disposition for most signals that terminate a process?
questions.option_a: A. SIG_IGN
questions.option_b: B. SIG_DFL
questions.option_c: C. SIG_TERM
questions.explanation: SIG_DFL sets the default disposition for a signal, which for most signals is to terminate the process upon receipt.

questions.question: In the str_cli function that writes two separate writes with a sleep in between, why does the second write generate SIGPIPE?
questions.option_a: A. Because the socket buffer is full
questions.option_b: B. Because the client's TCP has received an RST from the server during the sleep interval
questions.option_c: C. Because the server closed the connection normally
questions.option_d: D. Because the sleep() function interrupts the write
questions.explanation: When the server child is killed, it sends a FIN, and the client's write of the first byte may elicit an RST. During the sleep interval, this RST is received, making the second write attempt to a socket that has received an RST, generating SIGPIPE.

questions.question: What is the correct way to handle connect() if it returns EINTR?
questions.option_a: A. Call connect() again on the same socket
questions.option_b: B. Close the socket and create a new socket before calling connect() again
questions.option_c: C. Ignore the error and continue
questions.explanation: Unlike accept(), once connect() returns an error (including EINTR), you cannot call it again on the same socket. You must close the socket and create a new one before attempting to connect again.

questions.question: What happens to the data written by a client when the server host crashes after the write but before the data is acknowledged?
questions.option_a: A. The data is delivered when the server reboots
questions.option_b: B. The client's TCP buffers the data and retransmits until timeout
questions.option_c: C. The data is immediately discarded
questions.option_d: D. The client receives an immediate error
questions.explanation: When the server host crashes, the client's TCP buffers the data and continues retransmitting it, eventually timing out after multiple retransmissions (typically 9 minutes with 12 retries).

questions.question: What error does the client receive when the server host crashes and reboots before the client sends data?
questions.option_a: A. ETIMEDOUT
questions.option_b: B. ECONNRESET
questions.option_c: C. EHOSTUNREACH
questions.explanation: When a crashed server reboots, its TCP loses all information about previous connections. When the client sends data, the server TCP responds with an RST, causing the client's readline() to return ECONNRESET.

questions.question: During an orderly server host shutdown, what signals are sent to processes by the init process?
questions.option_a: A. SIGTERM followed by SIGKILL after a delay
questions.option_b: B. SIGKILL only
questions.option_c: C. SIGINT only
questions.explanation: During shutdown, init sends SIGTERM (catchable) to all processes, waits several seconds for cleanup, then sends SIGKILL (not catchable) to any remaining processes.

questions.question: What happens when a process writes to a socket after ignoring SIGPIPE?
questions.option_a: A. The write succeeds normally
questions.option_b: B. The write returns the EPIPE error
questions.option_c: C. The process is still terminated
questions.option_d: D. The socket is automatically reconnected
questions.explanation: If a process catches or ignores SIGPIPE, the write() operation returns EPIPE instead of generating the signal, allowing the application to handle the error gracefully.

questions.question: In the binary addition client-server example, why does the output '16777314' appear instead of the expected sum?
questions.option_a: A. Because of integer overflow
questions.option_b: B. Because of byte ordering differences between little-endian and big-endian systems
questions.option_c: C. Because of network congestion
questions.explanation: When passing binary structures between little-endian (Intel) and big-endian (SPARC) systems, the bytes are interpreted in opposite order, resulting in garbled values like 16777314 instead of the expected sum.

questions.question: What is the significance of the SO_KEEPALIVE socket option in detecting server host crashes?
questions.option_a: A. It keeps the connection alive indefinitely
questions.option_b: B. It periodically probes the connection to detect if the peer has crashed
questions.option_c: C. It prevents the socket from timing out
questions.option_d: D. It automatically reconnects broken connections
questions.explanation: SO_KEEPALIVE enables periodic probes to verify that the peer is still reachable, allowing detection of server host crashes even when the client is idle and blocked on input.

questions.question: What is the problem with using wait() in a SIGCHLD handler when multiple children terminate simultaneously?
questions.option_a: A. wait() cannot be called from a signal handler
questions.option_b: B. wait() only reaps one child per signal, but signals may not be queued
questions.option_c: C. wait() blocks indefinitely in signal handlers
questions.explanation: Since UNIX signals are not queued, multiple simultaneous SIGCHLD signals may be merged into one. If wait() is called only once, it will reap only one child, leaving the others as zombies.

questions.question: When a server child process terminates, what TCP segments are exchanged?
questions.option_a: A. The child sends a RST to the client
questions.option_b: B. The kernel closes the descriptors, sending FIN to the client, which responds with ACK
questions.option_c: C. The parent sends a FIN on behalf of the child
questions.explanation: When a child process terminates, the kernel automatically closes all its open descriptors, including the connected socket. This initiates TCP's connection termination with a FIN from the server, acknowledged by the client.

questions.question: Why must the parent process in a concurrent server call accept() in a loop that handles EINTR?
questions.option_a: A. To prevent infinite loops
questions.option_b: B. Because accept() might return EINTR when a SIGCHLD signal is caught during the blocking call
questions.option_c: C. To improve performance
questions.option_d: D. To handle multiple connections simultaneously
questions.explanation: When the parent is blocked in accept() and a SIGCHLD signal is delivered and caught, accept() may return EINTR. The loop with EINTR handling ensures the server continues accepting connections after signal handling.

questions.question: What is the correct sequence of system calls for a concurrent TCP server?
questions.option_a: A. socket(), bind(), connect(), accept()
questions.option_b: B. socket(), bind(), listen(), accept(), fork()
questions.option_c: C. socket(), listen(), bind(), accept()
questions.explanation: The correct sequence is: socket() to create endpoint, bind() to assign address, listen() to mark as passive, accept() to accept connection, then fork() to create child process for each client.

questions.question: What does the sigemptyset() function do in the signal handler setup?
questions.option_a: A. It empties the set of signals that will be blocked during signal handler execution
questions.option_b: B. It terminates all signals
questions.option_c: C. It resets the signal disposition
questions.explanation: sigemptyset() initializes the signal set to be empty, meaning no additional signals will be blocked while the signal handler is running (sa_mask is set to an empty set).

questions.question: Why is using text strings for numeric data exchange recommended over binary formats?
questions.option_a: A. Text strings are more efficient
questions.option_b: B. Text strings avoid byte ordering and data size issues between different architectures
questions.option_c: C. Binary formats are not supported by TCP
questions.option_d: D. Text strings are required by the socket API
questions.explanation: Text strings eliminate problems with different byte orders (endianness) and varying sizes of data types across architectures, ensuring interoperability as long as both hosts use the same character set.
